coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of orange emissive poly(o-phenylene diamine) nanoparticles and its application for solvents, pH and Ag+ sensing
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Patir, Khemnath
Gogoi, Sonit Kumar
coredata.dc:description: pH plays many essential functions in living organism while silver find applications in day to day life. However abnormal pH and improper dumping of silver ions can be lethal for living being. Herein, an orange emissive ploymer nanoparticles (OPNP) is synthesized by oxidative method at room temperature using 1, 2 diaminobenzene and potassium peroxodisulfate.The as obtained OPNP is characterized by TEM, FESEM, PXRD, FT-IR, EDX, UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. The fluorescence of the obtained OPNP is highly sensitive to solvents, pH and Ag+ ions. The fluorescent color of the OPNP is tuned from orange to bluish green (560–485 nm) by adjusting the polarity of the solvents. The fluorescence intensity of OPNP increases as the pH is increased from acidic (pH-2) to basic medium (pH-12). The fluorescence of the OPNP is quenched by Ag+ ions, hence it is employed for selective detection of Ag+ ions both in solid and aqueous phase. The calibration plot for Ag+ is linear in the 0–30 μM concentration ranges and its limit of detection is 0.398 μM, which is lower than the permitted limit of WHO. Furthermore, the fluorescent OPNP is explore for determination of Ag+ ion in environmental samples with good recoveries ranging from 95.83 to 103 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer Nanoparticles
Multicolor emission
pH sensor
Fluorescent turn off
Silver ions detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of orange emissive polymer nanoparticles (OPNP)
Fluorescence sensing of pH with filter paper strips
Selectivity test with OPNP filter paper strips

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: K. P thanks UGC-NFST-2015-17-ST-ASS-2321, Govt. of India, for the financial assistance. S.K.G is thankful to SERB-DST project SB/S1/PC-105/2012, UGC-DAE grant CRS/2021-22/01/410 and DST-PURSE for the financial assistance. The authors also thank SAIF, NEHU-Shillong, CIF, IIT-Guwahati and Department of Chemistry, Gauhati University for instrumental support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of OPNP. (b) HRTEM image of OPNP. (c) PXRD of OPNP. (d) FT-IR spectrum of OPNP.
(a) EDX spectrum of OPNP (inset: elemental composition). (b) FESEM image of OPNP. (c) Elemental mapping of OPNP with their corresponding elements (d) Carbon (e) Nitrogen (f) Oxygen.

(a) UV–visible spectrum of OPNP (black line), Normalized Fluorescence excitation spectrum of OPNP (redline) monitored at 560 nm, Normalized Fluorescence spectrum of OPNP (green line) under 420 nm excitation wavelength. (b) Fluorescence spectra of OPNP with variation of excitation wavelength.

(a) Fluorescence of OPNP in different solvents,water, ethanol, N,N dimethylformamide, acetone under day light (top) and 365 nm UV light irradiation (below). (b) UV–visible spectra of OPNP in different solvents,water, ethanol, N,N dimethylformamide and acetone. (c) Fluorescence spectra of OPNP in different solvents,water, ethanol, N,N dimethylformamide and acetone. (d) Corresponding color chromaticity diagram with CIE coordinates. Fluorescence spectra of OPNP in (e) Water (f) Ethanol (g) N,N dimethylformamide (h) Acetone.

(a) OPNP solution with different pH (2–12) under daylight. (b) Fluorescence of OPNP solution with different pH (2–12) under 365 nm UV irradiation. (c) UV–visible spectra of OPNP solution with different pH (2–12). (d) Fluorescence spectra of OPNP solution with different pH (2–12). (e) Normalized fluorescence spectra of OPNP solution with different pH (2–12). (f) Plot of fluorescence intensity versus pH. (g) OPNP filter paper strips with different pH (2–12) under daylight. (h) Fluorescence of OPNP filter paper strips with different pH (2–12) under 356 nm UV light irradiation.

(a) OPNP solution in presence of different metal ions under daylight. (b) Fluorescence of OPNP solution in presence of different metal ions under 365 nm UV irradiation. (c) OPNP filter paper strips in presence of different metal ions under daylight. (d) Fluorescence of OPNP filter paper strips in presence of different metal ions under 365 nm UV irradiation.

(a) UV–visible spectra of OPNP solution in presence of different metal ions. (b) Fluorescence spectra of OPNP solution in presence of different metal ions under 420 nm excitation wavelength. (c) Fluorescence spectra of OPNP with increasing Ag+ concentration from 0 μM to 55 μM. (d) Corresponding F0/F versus Ag+ concentration. (e) Fluorescence lifetime decay curve of OPNP in absence and presence of 50 μM Ag+. (f) Fluorescence spectra of OPNP solution in presence of different metal ions + Ag+ under 420 nm excitation wavelength.
Synthetic scheme of OPNP and its applications.
Determination of Ag+ in real tap and lake water.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of orange emissive poly(o-phenylene diamine) nanoparticles and its application for solvents, pH and Ag+ sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Gauhati, G. B. Nagar, Guwahati 781014, Assam, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Gauhati, G. B. Nagar

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Gauhati, G. B. Nagar, Guwahati 781014, Assam, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Science and Humanities, Assam University, Silchar 788011, Assam, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Science and Humanities

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Science and Humanities, Assam University, Silchar 788011, Assam, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: pH plays many essential functions in living organism while silver find applications in day to day life. However abnormal pH and improper dumping of silver ions can be lethal for living being. Herein, an orange emissive ploymer nanoparticles (OPNP) is synthesized by oxidative method at room temperature using 1, 2 diaminobenzene and potassium peroxodisulfate.The as obtained OPNP is characterized by TEM, FESEM, PXRD, FT-IR, EDX, UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. The fluorescence of the obtained OPNP is highly sensitive to solvents, pH and Ag+ ions. The fluorescent color of the OPNP is tuned from orange to bluish green (560–485 nm) by adjusting the polarity of the solvents. The fluorescence intensity of OPNP increases as the pH is increased from acidic (pH-2) to basic medium (pH-12). The fluorescence of the OPNP is quenched by Ag+ ions, hence it is employed for selective detection of Ag+ ions both in solid and aqueous phase. The calibration plot for Ag+ is linear in the 0–30 μM concentration ranges and its limit of detection is 0.398 μM, which is lower than the permitted limit of WHO. Furthermore, the fluorescent OPNP is explore for determination of Ag+ ion in environmental samples with good recoveries ranging from 95.83 to 103 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent conducting polymer materials (CP) are new class of polymer materials which have attracted to increasing attention due to its easy synthetic process, high optical absorptivity, excellent photostability, low cost and low toxicity [1–3]. Due to this superior properties fluorescent conducting polymers (CP) such as poly (o-phenylenediamine) (PoPD), Polyaniline (PANI), polythiophene (PTH), polyparaphenylene (PPP), polypyridine (PPY) are widely applied as fluorescent sensors, electroluminescent materials etc. [4–8]. Fluorescent poly (phenylenediamine) has various advantages such as simple and cost effective synthetic process, highly sensitive, excellent selectivity and anti-interference ability [9–11] Hence, poly (phenylenediamine) based materials are explored as fluorescent sensors for detecting different analytes. Various fluorescent conducting polymer nanoparticles such as coordination polymer colloids, poly (o-phenylenediamine) (PoPD), poly (m-phenylenediamine) (PmPD) are synthesized and applied as sensors for detection of nucleic acid [12–14]. Fluorescent poly (p-phenylenediamine) nanofibers are used for detection of caffeine and thiols [11,15]. In addition heterodoped fluorescent poly (o-phenylenediamine) nanospheres are applied for sensing Cu2+, Ca2+, Hg2+ and H2O2 [16].

Intracellular pH plays many essential and important roles in living organism such as cell growth and death, metabolism of organs, ions transport [17–19]. The acidification of soil and water resources, used of food additives is also threating the survival of living being [20,21]. In recent times, the rapid industrial development is causing heavy metal pollution which has become worldwide problems as it posed threats to organisms and environments [22]. Silver find applications in our daily life, such as photographic, electrical and pharmaceutical industries due to its multifunctional nature [23]. As a result silver ions is released to the environment poisoning aquatic animals, plants and human being. As silver ions has the affinity of binding to various metabolites and enzymes, results in abnormality in their functions [24]. Thus a high accuracy method for sensing the pH values and silver ions are necessary in biocatalysis, environment monitoring and food safety. Various methods are used for detection of silver ions include inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS), stripping voltammetry, electrochemical sensors, atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) and potentiometric methods [25–28]. However, most of these methods involve complex sample preparation, costly instruments and time consuming operations. The fluorometric and colorimetric methods could overcome these issues, since it is convenient to use due to their simplicity, high sensitivity, non-invasive and real time detection. As such fluorescent probes for detection of silver such as organic dyes, metal nanoparticles, fluorescent proteins and semiconductor qauntum dots are reported but suffer from few drawbacks such as complex synthesis process, poor solubility in aqueous medium, toxicity and low selectivity [19,21,24,29,30]. Therefore, development of new colorimetry and fluorescent probes that can detect pH values and silver ions sensitively and selectively by a simple synthetic approach is needed for the environmental remediation and food security.

In this work, we have developed an one pot oxidative synthesis of orange emissive ploymer nanoparticles (OPNP) at room temperature. The fluorescence of the obtained OPNP is highly sensitive to solvents, pH and Ag+ ions. The fluorescent color of the OPNP is tuned from orange to bluish green with decreasing polarity of the solvents. The pH dependent fluorescence of OPNP is demonstrated from acidic to basic medium. Moreover, the fluorescence of the OPNP is is employed for detection of Ag+ ions due to its higher selectivity towards Ag+ ions than other metal ions. The UV–visible and fluorescence lifetime measurements indicates the mechanism of fluorescence sensing of Ag+ ions to be static in nature. Hence, these fluorescent OPNP may be applied as sensor for analytes in environmental samples.

1, 2 diaminobenzene was purchased from LOBA Chemie Pvt. Ltd and used without further purification. Manganese sulfate, mercury chloride, copper sulfate, nickel sulfate, zinc sulfate, cobalt chloride, sodium chloride, ferric chloride, cadmium chloride, magnesium sulfate, silver nitrate, calcium chloride, sulphuric acid, sodium hydroxide and potassium peroxodisulfate were received from Merck Specialities Private Limited, India. Double distilled water is used throughout the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of orange emissive polymer nanoparticles (OPNP)
ce:section.ce:para: 10 mL of 0.2 Molar 1, 2 diaminobenzene and 0.5 mL of 0.025 Molar potassium peroxodisulfate in aqueous dispersion was mixed in a beaker and was kept at room temperature overnight. The obtained orange product is centrifuge and resulting precipitate is collected and used for further analysis

Firstly stock solution of OPNP is prepared by dissolving 0.01 g of OPNP in 100 mL water or other solvents. 2 mL of OPNP from the each solution are transferred to the cuvette and its fluorescence spectra are recorded with 420 nm excitation wavelength at room temperature keeping both excitation and emission wavelength slit 5 nm.

Firstly stock solution of OPNP is prepared by dissolving 0.01 g of OPNP in 100 mL water and the pH are adjusted using sulphuric acid or sodium hydroxide. 2 mL of OPNP with different pH solution are transferred to the cuvette and its fluorescence spectra are recorded with 420 nm excitation wavelength at room temperature keeping both excitation and emission wavelength slit 5 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of pH with filter paper strips
ce:section.ce:para: Filter paper was cut into rectangular piece of 6 cm × 1 cm. Filter paper pieces were immersed to OPNP solution with different pH for 5 min and is allow to dry at room temperature. After drying the fluorescence of filter papers are photographed under a UV-lamp with 365 nm irradiation.

Firstly stock solution of OPNP is prepared by dissolving 0.01 g of OPNP in 100 mL water. 10 μL OPNP is mixed to 1 mL of water and different concentrations of 2 μL of Ag+ is added. After that the fluorescence spectra of the mixture solution are recorded with 420 nm excitation wavelength at room temperature keeping both the excitation and emission slit 5 nm.

The selectivity of OPNP towards Ag+ ions, compared to other metals ions such as Mg2+ , Cd2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+ , Co2+, Hg2+, Ca2+ and Zn2+ is verified under identical conditions. 50 μM solutions (2 μL) of different metal ions are mixed with 1 mL of OPNP. After that the fluorescence spectra of the mixture solution are recorded with 420 nm excitation wavelength at room temperature keeping both the excitation and emission slit 5 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity test with OPNP filter paper strips
ce:section.ce:para: Filter paper was cut into rectangular piece of 6 cm × 1 cm. Filter paper pieces are immersed to OPNP solution with different metal ions (50 μM) for 5 min and allow to dry at room temperature. After drying the fluorescence of filter papers are photographed under a UV-lamp with 365 nm irradiation.

The Powder XRD pattern of the sample is recorded from 5° to 80° 2θ with Rigaku Ultima- IV X-ray diffraction measurement system with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.54 Ǻ) at room temperature. UV 1800 spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU, Japan) was used for recording UV–vis absorption (200 to 1000 nm). FT-IR spectra was recorded in SHIMADZU IR Affinity, in the range from (400 to 4000) cm−1 with KBr pellets. Fluorescence measurements were carried out with Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer (Japan).Time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra were measured using Eddinburg Instruments, Model: FSP920. The morphologies and particle size distribution of the sample is observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-2100, JEOL) with an accelerating voltage of 60–200 kV. The elemental mapping and energy dispersive x-ray (EDX) of the samples were observed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (Model-Sigma 300, Zeiss). The fluorescence quantum yield of OPNP (QY) was measured by established procedure. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY = 0.54 %) was taken as standard reference. The QY is calculated by the following equation.
(1)
Q
OPNP
=
Q S
×
F
OPNP
/
F S
×
A S
/
A
OPNP
×
η
OPNP
/
η S
2
Where, S signify standard. F is the measured integrated fluorescence intensity, A is the absorbance and ɳ is the refractive index of the solvent, ɳOPNP = ɳS=1.33.

The orange emissive polymer nanoparticles are synthesized directly by oxidation of 1, 2 diaminobenzene with potassium peroxodisulfate according to the scheme 1 . The morphology and particle size distribution of the OPNP is observed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1
a and b, the OPNP are mostly spherical in shape with average particle size of 45 nm (Fig. 1 a, inset). The powder X-ray diffraction pattern of the OPNP displays broad peak at 19.860 2θ value indicating its amorphous nature (Fig. 1 c) [16]. FT-IR analysis (Fig. 1 d) gives the information of functional groups presence on the OPNP. The two peaks observed at 2922 cm−1 and 3441 cm−1 could be assigned to be –C-H and –NH2 stretching vibrations [31]. The peaks at 1631 cm−1 and 1381 cm−1 are due to –C=C- and −C-N stretching modes respectively [32]. Moreover, band at 1047 cm−1 assigned to be −C-O vibrations while the peaks at 673 cm−1 and 753 cm−1 are due to characteristic bending vibrations of benzene structure in the phenazine skeleton [9].

The elemental composition of OPNP is analysis with energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) and its elemental mapping. As shown in Fig. 2
a, the OPNP consists of C=64.58 %, N=34.75 % and O=0.67 %. This results is further confirmed by their respective elemental mapping, Fig. 2c-f. As seen from the FESEM image of OPNP, Fig. 2 b, the particle shape of the OPNP are mostly spherical with size below 100 nm, substantiating the TEM results.

In order to explore the optical properties of OPNP, the UV–visible and fluorescence spectra are recorded. The UV–visible spectrum of OPNP (Fig. 3
a, black line) show two peaks at 290 nm and 420 nm which are assigned to be π-π* and n-π* transitions respectively [13]. Fig. 3 a (Red and green line) shows the excitation and emission sepctra of OPNP. The excitation spectra of OPNP displays two peaks at 280 nm and 405 nm, similar to the UV–visible spectrum. The OPNP show fluorescence emission centre at 560 nm under excitation wavelength of 420 nm. As we can observed from the Fig. 3 b, the fluorescence emission centre at 560 nm does not show obvious shift when the excitation wavelength is change from 360 nm to 520 nm, however its fluorescence intensity decreases continuously with increasing wavelength.This results indicates that the OPNP show excitation wavelength independent fluorescence behaviour.

The solubility of the OPNP is tested in four solvents, water, ethanol, N,N dimethylformamide (DMF) and acetone. OPNP is soluble in all this solvents and interstingly it displays different fluorescence color at same exctation wavelength depending on the polarity of the solvents. The OPNP shows orange fluorescence (560 nm) in water, yellow fluorescence (530 nm) in ethanol, green fluorescence in DMF (515 nm) and bluish fluorescence (485 nm) in acetone under same 420 nm excitation wavelength, which can be observed from digital photograph (Fig. 4
a) as well as from the normalized fluorescence spectra (Fig. 4 c). Fig. 4 b, displays the UV–visible spectra of OPNP with different solvents. The absorption peaks are observed at 417.9 nm, 427 nm, 422 nm and 419 nm for water, ethanol, DMF and acetone respectively. The inconsistency of UV–visible results of OPNP in terms of polarity of the solvents may be due to hydrogen bonding (HB) along with internal charge transfers, conformational changes etc. [33]. According to molecular fluorescence principle the mechanism of spectral shift are of two types (1) general solvent effect- only depends on the polarity change of the solvents and (2) specific solvents effect- it involves several complex factors such as hydrogen bond (HB) between the probes and the solvents, internal charge transfers, probe-probe interactions, conformational change etc. [34,35]. The UV–visible and fluorescence results of the OPNP indicates that specific solvents effect is more suitable to correlate its observation. The change of fluorescence colour from orange to bluish green is also observable from the colour chromaticity diagram along with their coordinates, Fig. 4 d. In addition, we recorded the fluorescence spectra of OPNP with different solvents, water, ethanol, DMF and acetone with variation of excitation wavlength from 360 nm to 520 nm. In all this solvents, water, ethanol, DMF and acetone the OPNP fluorescence emission centre remains at 560 nm, 530 nm, 515 nm and 485 nm respectively thought the excitation wavelength is changed from 360 nm to 520 nm, confirming the excitation wavelength independent fluorescence behaviour (Fig. 4 e-h).

Fig. 5 , show the colorimetric and fluorescence changes of OPNP with changing pH. At pH < 7, the UV–visible absorption peak of OPNP at 420 nm is redshifted to 449 nm while at pH > 7 the peak at 420 nm is blue shifted to 415 nm (Fig. 5 c). The visible colour of the OPNP solution changed from deep yellow colour to light colour with increasing pH from 2 to 12, Fig. 5 a. The fluorescence responses of the OPNP solution is performed and is shown in Fig. 5 d. Under acidic condition (pH 1–6) the emission peak was obtained at 565 nm under the 420 nm excitation with fluorescence intensity deminished, Fig. 5 b and d. For the the pH ranging from 7 to 12 the fluorescence emission intensity increases and its peak is blue shifted to 556 nm from 560 nm which is visible from normalized fluorescence spectra (Fig. 5 e).The fluorescence intensity at 560 nm is plotted as a function of pH in Fig. 5 f. The fluorescence of the OPNP solutions under 365 nm UV irradiation display weak orange to strong fluorescence when the pH is increased from 2 to 12, Fig. 5 b. Under extremely acidic conditions, the fluorescence is quenched. As shown in Fig. 5 g, the colour of the pH test strips loaded with OPNP under visible light varied from deep yellow colour to light yellow colour with increasing pH from 2 to 12. For the same pH test paper strips loaded with OPNP under 356 nm UV light irradiation show very weak orange fluorescence to strong orange fluorescence as the pH is increased from 2 to 12, Fig. 5 h. Therefore, this OPNP fluorerscent materials is suitable for detecting acidic pH.

In order to explore possible metal ions detection, we have carried out the UV visible and fluorescence quenching experiments in prescence of 50 μM concentration of different metal ions, Mg2+ , Cd2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+ , Co2+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+ and Ag+ under day light and 365 nm UV light irradiation. Interestingly, we have found that the fluorescence of OPNP is significantly quenched only for Ag+ out of all the tested metal ions, Fig. 6 . As shown in Fig. 6 a, under visible light the colour of the OPNP solution in presence of Mg2+ , Cd2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+ , Co2+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+ remain same. However, the colour of the OPNP solution in presence of Ag+ changes to dark brown. Similar observation is obtained for filter paper strips loaded with OPNP, Fig. 6 c. As we can observed from both the OPNP solution and filter paper strips loaded with OPNP in presence of different metal ions, under 365 nm UV light irradiation, the fluorescence of OPNP is quenched only for Ag+, Fig. 6 b and d.

In order to confirmed our visual observation, we have recorded the UV–visible and fluorescence spectra of OPNP solution in prescence of the different metal ions. As shown in Fig. 7
a and b, the UV–visible spectra and fluorescence of OPNP solution in prescence of Ag+ display significant changes out of all the tested metal ions. The fluorescence selectivity of OPNP towards Ag+ is clearly observed from the spectral changes, Fig. 7 b. In Fig. 7 c, quantitative determination of Ag+ concentrations is performed. When the Ag+ concentration is increases from 0 μM to 55 μM, the fluorescence emission at 560 nm decreases continuously. A good linear relationship is obtained by the plot of Ag+ versus F0 /F in the range, 0 μM to 30 μM (where F0 is the fluorescence intensity of the OPNP at 560 nm without Ag+ ions, F is the fluorescence intensity of the OPNP at 560 nm with Ag+ ions), Fig. 7 d. The fluorescence quenching of OPNP in presence of Ag+ is described by the following Stern Volmer equation (2).
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K S
A
g +
Where, KSV is the Stern Volmer constant.

The linear regression equation obtained by fitting the above Stern Volmer equation is y = 0.5053 + 0.928 (R2 = 0.986). The limit of detection (LOD) is determined based on the formula, LOD=3s/k, where s and k are the standard deviation of the fluorescence of blank OPNP and slope from the regression equation respectively. The limit of detection of Ag+ is found to be 0.398 μM which is less than the permitted limit of 0.95 μM by world health organization (WHO) [36]. The fluorescence lifetime decay curve of OPNP in absence and presence of 50 μM Ag+ is shown in Fig. 7 e. The average fluorescence lifetime before (2.33 ns) and after addition of 50 μM Ag+ (2.72 ns) remain almost same indicating the fluorescence mechanism to be static in nature [37–39]. As previously observed from the UV–visible spectra of the OPNP in presence of Ag+ changes significantly, Fig. 7 a, further proving that the OPNP form complex with the Ag+ ions. In addition interference of the other metal ions to Ag+ is tested by carry out the fluorescence measurements of the OPNP in presence of the Ag+ and other metal ions with fixed concentration of 50 μM. As we observed from the Fig. 7 f, there is no significant influence on the fluorescence of OPNP in mixture of Ag+ and other metal ions. This results indicates that the OPNP is highly specific and selective towards Ag+ ion. Therefore, this fluorescent OPNP materials can be easily applied for detection of Ag+ real samples collected from local area, as shown in Table 1 . The tap and lake water are spiked with Ag+ and different concentration of 2, 4 and 6 μM followed by fluorescence measurements [40–42]. The good recoveries are obtained in the ranges of 95.83 % to 103 % for three replicate experiments with relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 2.93 % which indicates that the OPNP have the ability to detect Ag+ ion in real environmental samples.

In summary, one step oxidative method is developed for synthesizing fluorescent polymer nanoparticles (OPNP) with qauntum yield of 4.85 % using 1, 2 diaminobenzene and potassium peroxodisulfate as the starting materials. The fluorescence color of OPNP is tunable from orange to bluish green based on the polarity of the dispersion solvents medium. Moreover the fluorescent of OPNP is sensitive to acidic pH and Ag+. The concentration dependent sensing of Ag+ ion is performed in the ranges, 0 μM to 55 μM and its linear ranges is found to be 0 μM- 30 μM, with limit of detection of 0.398 μM which is lesser than WHO permitted limit. In addition recoveries of Ag+ in both tap and lake water are found to in the ranges, 95.83 to 103 %, making these fluorescent OPNP as a suitable materials to be used as a sensor for detecting Ag+ in environmental samples.

Khemnath Patir: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Sonit Kumar Gogoi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: K. P thanks UGC-NFST-2015-17-ST-ASS-2321, Govt. of India, for the financial assistance. S.K.G is thankful to SERB-DST project SB/S1/PC-105/2012, UGC-DAE grant CRS/2021-22/01/410 and DST-PURSE for the financial assistance. The authors also thank SAIF, NEHU-Shillong, CIF, IIT-Guwahati and Department of Chemistry, Gauhati University for instrumental support.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multicolor conjugated polymer dots for biological fluorescence imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Wu, B. Bull, C. Szymanski, K. Christensen, J. McNeill, Multicolor conjugated polymer dots for biological fluorescence imaging, ACS Nano. 2 (11) (2008) 2415-2423. https://doi.org/10.1021/nn800590n.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A compact and highly fluorescent orange-emitting polymer dot for specific subcellular imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. M. Ye, C. F. Wu, Y. H. Jin, M. Wang, Y. H. Chan, J. B. Yu, W. Sun, S. Hayden, D. T. Chiu, A compact and highly fluorescent orange-emitting polymer dot for specific subcellular imaging, Chem. Commun. 48 (12) (2012) 1778-1780. https://doi.org/10.1039/C2CC16486H.
D. Tuncel, H. V. Demir, Conjugated polymer nanoparticles, Nanoscale. 2 (4) (2010) 484-494. https://doi.org/10.1039/B9NR00374F.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Morphology controllable fabrication of poly-o-phenylenediamine microstructures tuned by the ionic strength and their applications in pH sensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Jiang, S. Ma, H. Bi, D. Chen, X. Han, Morphology controllable fabrication of poly-o-phenylenediamine microstructures tuned by the ionic strength and their applications in pH sensors, J. Mater. Chem. A. 2 (45) (2014) 19208-19213. https://doi.org/10.1039/C4TA04269G.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent polyaniline nanoclips (PANCs): A highly sensitive and selective chemical sensor for the detection of Hg (II) ions in aqueous media

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Marieeswaran, P. Panneerselvam, Fluorescent polyaniline nanoclips (PANCs): a highly sensitive and selective chemical sensor for the detection of Hg (II) ions in aqueous media, Chemistry Select 5 (15) (2020) 4481-4487. https://doi.org/10.1002/slct.202000545.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new polythiophene derivative-based fluorescent sensor for Co2+, Cu2+, Cd2+ and its complex with Cu2+ for sensing homocysteine and glutathione

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Guo, P. Li, M. Pei, G. Zhang, A new polythiophene derivative-based fluorescent sensor for Co2+, Cu2+, Cd2+ and its complex with Cu2+ for sensing homocysteine and glutathione, Sens. Actuators B: Chem. 221 (2015) 1223-1228. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.07.105.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reversible and highly selective fluorescent sensor for mercury (II) based on a water-soluble poly(para-phenylene)s containing thymine and sulfonate moieties

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Lv, C. Ouyang, X. Yin, H. Zheng, Z. Zuo, J. Xu, H. Liu, Y. Rapid Commun. 29 (19) (2008) 1588-1592. https://doi.org/10.1002/marc.200800256.
